mnemonics.title: Class Ranges
mnemonics.content: A(1-126), B(128-191), C(192-223), D(224-239), E(240-255). Remember: 'Any Body Can Do Everything' - A=Any(1), B=Body(128), C=Can(192), D=Do(224), E=Everything(240)

mnemonics.title: Subnet Mask Bits
mnemonics.content: Count 1s in subnet mask, multiply by 8 to get CIDR. Example: 255.255.255.192 has 8+8+8+2=26 bits = /26.

mnemonics.title: Usable Hosts Formula
mnemonics.content: Usable hosts = 2^(32-CIDR) - 2. Always subtract 2 for network and broadcast addresses.

mnemonics.title: Private IP Classes
mnemonics.content: Remember: 10.x.x.x (big networks), 172.16-31.x.x (medium), 192.168.x.x (small). Think: 10 is for Large, 172.16 is Medium, 192.168 is Small.

acronyms.usage: The 32-bit addressing scheme used for network identification
Method of allocating IP addresses and routing prefix notation (e.g., /24)
Allows different subnet sizes within the same network
Automatically assigns IP addresses to network devices
Maps private IPs to public IPs for internet access
Maps IP addresses to MAC addresses on local network
Translates domain names to IP addresses

visualTips.visualization: Think of a 32-bit address as 4 houses (octets), each with 8 rooms (bits). Each house number (0-255) represents one octet.
Imagine the network portion as the street name and host portion as house numbers. Subnet mask tells you where the street ends and house numbers begin.
Each octet position represents: 128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1. Add up the values where bit is 1. Example: 11001010 = 128+64+8+2 = 202.
Borrowing bits from host portion is like dividing a building into floors. Each borrowed bit doubles the number of subnets but halves hosts per subnet.

analogies.analogy: Like a postal address: Network portion is the street/city, host portion is the house number. Mail (data) needs both to be delivered.
Like a sorting machine that separates letters by region (network) from individual deliveries (hosts).
Like a post office that knows which networks it serves and forwards packets to other networks.
Like sending a notice to every house on a street - everyone receives it but only one responds.
